1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: true
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am mandatory every plan phase. The audit warning fires if I am
16	    skipped.
17	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
18	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
19	    chapter view is the point of running me.
20	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
21	    need a blueprint-writer dispatch this iter. You do not need to
22	    re-read the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
23	
24	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
25	
26	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
27	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
28	
29	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
30	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
31	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C (the `Foo/Bar.lean →
32	    Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). Look up C in my per-chapter checklist.
33	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
34	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
35	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
36	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
37	    blueprint depends on):
38	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives. Defer the prover round on F
39	       to the next iter.
40	    2. Dispatch a `blueprint-writer` for C THIS iter with a directive
41	       targeting the specific must-fix items I flagged.
42	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was deferred (cite the
43	       reviewer findings).
44	  - Re-dispatching me after the writer returns is optional within the
45	    same iter — the next iter's mandatory dispatch of me will confirm.
46	
47	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
48	
49	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one or
50	    more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch blueprint-writers
51	    to fill those gaps in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin
52	    work on a route until its blueprint coverage is in place.
53	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
54	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
55	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
56	    in the blueprint.
57	
58	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
59	
60	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
61	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
62	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
63	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
64	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
65	---
66	
67	# Blueprint Reviewer
68	
69	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
70	
71	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
72	
73	## Your Job
74	
75	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
76	
77	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
78	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
79	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
80	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
81	
82	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
83	
84	## Always read everything
85	
86	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
87	
88	## Directive Format
89	
90	```markdown
91	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
92	
93	## Slug
94	<slug>
95	
96	## Strategy snapshot
97	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy.>
98	
99	## Routes
100	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
101	
102	## References
103	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
104	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
105	
106	## Focus areas (optional)
107	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
108	
109	## Known issues
110	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
111	```
112	
113	## What you do
114	
115	1. **Read your directive completely.**
116	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
117	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
118	   - Read the entire chapter.
119	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
120	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
121	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
122	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
123	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
124	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
125	7. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
126	
127	You may also use:
128	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
129	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
130	
131	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
132	
133	## Report format
134	
135	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
136	
137	```markdown
138	# Blueprint Review Report
139	
140	## Slug
141	<slug>
142	
143	## Iteration
144	<NNN>
145	
146	## Top-level summaries
147	
148	### Incomplete parts
149	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
150	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
151	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
152	
153	### Proofs lacking detail
154	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
155	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
156	
157	### Lean difficulty quality
158	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
159	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
160	
161	### Multi-route coverage
162	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
163	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
164	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
165	
166	## Per-chapter
167	
168	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
169	- **complete**: true | partial | false
170	- **correct**: true | partial | false
171	- **notes**:
172	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
173	  - ...
174	
175	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex
176	- **complete**: ...
177	- **correct**: ...
178	- **notes**:
179	  - ...
180	
181	(One block per chapter. Cover every chapter, including the ones that
182	look fine — they get `complete: true`, `correct: true`, `notes: -`.)
183	
184	## Cross-chapter notes
185	
186	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
187	- `<chapter A>` `\lean{Foo.bar}` references a declaration that no longer exists in `Foo.lean` (renamed?).
188	- ...
189	
190	(Use this section for findings that span multiple chapters. Omit if empty.)
191	
192	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
193	
194	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here. These take precedence over everything else; the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md before any Lean work this iter.
195	
196	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>. Resolving requires either changing the strategy or redefining `foo`.
197	
198	## Severity summary
199	
200	Apply these rules verbatim — they decide whether the plan agent dispatches a blueprint-writer this iter or defers.
201	
202	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
203	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
204	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
205	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter. A `partial` chapter cannot be relied on by any prover; a blueprint-writer must be dispatched.
206	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
207	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels — these silently corrupt the dependency graph and must be fixed before provers downstream of them run.
208	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
209	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
210	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues, missing `\texttt{...}` decoration mentions, etc.
211	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
212	
213	Overall verdict: one sentence.
214	```
215	
216	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter (see dispatcher_notes for the gate rule). Do not under-classify to avoid blocking provers — the project pays more when a prover formalizes against a wrong/incomplete blueprint than when the prover waits one iter for a writer to land the fix.
217	
218	## Return value
219	
220	Your final assistant message:
221	
222	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings`
223	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, etc.)
224	- The path to your full report.
225	
226	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
227	
228	## Reminders
229	
230	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
231	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
232	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
233	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.
234	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format.
235

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions

1	\chapter{The symmetric monoidal category of $\mathcal O_X$-modules}
2	\label{chap:Modules_Monoidal}
3	
4	This chapter opens Phase~C by introducing the symmetric monoidal structure on the category of $\mathcal O_X$-modules for a scheme $X$. It is a prerequisite for Chapter~\ref{chap:Picard_LineBundle}: the definition of a \emph{line bundle} as an invertible object requires a monoidal category to invert in. Mathlib provides the structure on the presheaf side (\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}) and the sheafification machinery (\texttt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}); the gap addressed here is the transfer of the monoidal structure across sheafification to the sheaf side, yielding an instance \texttt{MonoidalCategory $X$.Modules}.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition and lemma here is auxiliary, supplying the Mathlib-gap-fill that unblocks the refinement of $\mathrm{LineBundle}$ from a global-sections approximation to the geometric definition.
7	
8	\section{Setup}
9	
10	Fix a scheme $X$. Recall (Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}) that $\mathcal O_X$-modules are organised as a category $X.\mathrm{Modules}$, packaged as $\mathrm{SheafOfModules}(\mathcal O_X)$ where $\mathcal O_X$ is regarded as a sheaf of (commutative) rings on the underlying topological space of $X$. The presheaf-of-modules variant $\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}(\mathcal O_X)$ already carries a symmetric monoidal structure in Mathlib (\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}), with tensor product computed pointwise on each open: for two presheaves of modules $M, N$,
11	\[
12	  (M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N)(U) \;=\; M(U) \otimes_{\mathcal O_X(U)} N(U).
13	\]
14	The associator, unitors, and braiding are inherited pointwise from $\mathrm{ModuleCat}\,\mathcal O_X(U)$.
15	
16	\section{Tensor product of sheaves of modules}
17	
18	\begin{definition}\leanok
19	[Tensor product of $\mathcal O_X$-modules]
20	  \label{def:Modules_tensorObj}
21	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
22	  Let $X$ be a scheme and $M, N \in X.\mathrm{Modules}$. The \emph{tensor product} $M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N$ is defined as the sheafification of the presheaf tensor product:
23	  \[
24	    M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N \;:=\; \mathrm{sheafify}\bigl(M_{\mathrm{psh}} \otimes_{\mathcal O_X, \mathrm{psh}} N_{\mathrm{psh}}\bigr),
25	  \]
26	  where $(-)_{\mathrm{psh}}$ is the forgetful functor $X.\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X$, and the inner tensor product is the pointwise one supplied by Mathlib.
27	\end{definition}
28	
29	\begin{remark}
30	  When $X$ is affine, both factors are quasi-coherent and the presheaf tensor product is already a sheaf on the basis of basic opens, so the sheafification step collapses to an isomorphism; but for a general scheme $X$ (and a general pair of $\mathcal O_X$-modules) the presheaf tensor product is genuinely not a sheaf and sheafification is essential.
31	\end{remark}
32	
33	\begin{theorem}\leanok
34	[Symmetric monoidal structure on $X.\mathrm{Modules}$]
35	  \label{thm:Modules_MonoidalCategory}
36	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.instMonoidalCategory}
37	  \uses{def:Modules_tensorObj}
38	  For every scheme $X$, the category $X.\mathrm{Modules}$ carries a canonical symmetric monoidal structure with tensor product given by Definition~\ref{def:Modules_tensorObj} and tensor unit $\mathcal O_X$ itself (as the trivial $\mathcal O_X$-module).
39	\end{theorem}
40	
41	\begin{proof}
42	  \uses{def:Modules_tensorObj}
43	  \leanok
44	  Set $L := \mathrm{sheafification}$, regarded as the left adjoint $\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X \to X.\mathrm{Modules}$, and let $F$ denote its right adjoint (the forgetful functor). The right adjoint $F$ is fully faithful, so by general localisation theory the localisation $L \dashv F$ realises $X.\mathrm{Modules}$ as the localisation of $\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X$ at the class $W := L^{-1}(\mathrm{Iso})$ of morphisms inverted by sheafification.
45	
46	  We claim $W$ is \emph{monoidal} in the sense of \cite{CategoryTheory.LocalizedMonoidal}: it is stable under left and right whiskering by any presheaf of modules. Indeed, if $L(f)$ is an iso, then for any presheaf of modules $X_0$,
47	  \[
48	    L(X_0 \,\triangleleft\, f) \;=\; L(X_0) \,\triangleleft\, L(f),
49	  \]
50	  since sheafification is a left adjoint and hence preserves colimits, and the presheaf tensor bifunctor preserves colimits in each variable (the pointwise tensor is computed in $\mathrm{ModuleCat}\,\mathcal O_X(U)$, which is cocomplete). The right-hand side is an iso because $L(f)$ is. Symmetrically for right whiskering.
51	
52	  With $W$ monoidal, the standard \texttt{LocalizedMonoidal} construction equips the localisation $X.\mathrm{Modules} = L(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X)/W$ with a unique symmetric monoidal structure making $L$ a symmetric monoidal functor. The tensor product on the sheaf side is computed as $L$ applied to the presheaf-side tensor, which matches Definition~\ref{def:Modules_tensorObj}. The associator/unitors/braiding are images of their presheaf counterparts under $L$, and the pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence axioms transfer by functoriality of $L$ and the universal property of localisation.
53	\end{proof}
54	
55	\begin{remark}[Mathlib precedent]
56	  Mathlib has the same pattern at one level of abstraction higher: \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.monoidalCategory} lifts a symmetric monoidal structure from a value category $A$ to $\mathrm{Sheaf}\,J\,A$ provided $J.W$ is monoidal and $A$ has weak sheafification ($J.W$-isomorphisms are detected pointwise after sheafification). The condensed case (\texttt{LightCondensed.instMonoidalCategorySheafLightProfiniteCoherentTopologyModuleCat}) shows this pattern in action. The wrinkle in our situation is that the value category varies with the open set (we have a sheaf of rings, not a fixed ring), so the generic instance \texttt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory} does not apply directly --- instead we adapt the parallel \texttt{LocalizedMonoidal} pattern to the presheaf-of-modules formalism by establishing $W.\texttt{IsMonoidal}$ for $W := L^{-1}(\mathrm{Iso})$. The proof is bookkeeping rather than mathematics: colimit-preservation of left adjoints applied to the (separately colimit-preserving) presheaf-tensor bifunctor.
57	\end{remark}
58	
59	\begin{remark}[Status of $W.\mathrm{IsMonoidal}$ and the stalks-level argument]
60	  Mathematically, $W.\mathrm{IsMonoidal}$ is the statement: \emph{if $L(f)$ is an isomorphism in $X.\mathrm{Modules}$, then so is $L(g \triangleleft f)$ for any presheaf of modules $g$} (and symmetrically for right whiskering). Concretely, $L(f)$ is iso iff $f$ becomes iso on each stalk (since sheafification is local), so we must show that $g \triangleleft f$ is stalkwise iso. At the stalk $x$, the presheaf tensor product computes as the $\mathcal O_{X,x}$-balanced tensor product $g_x \otimes_{\mathcal O_{X,x}} f_x$; tensoring with the identity on $g_x$ preserves stalk-level isomorphisms (since base change along a flat module factor preserves isos). This is the stalks-level argument the Lean side is attempting (iter-080 Lane 3). The bookkeeping required is non-trivial because Mathlib does not yet expose the stalk-of-presheaf-tensor identification in the varying-ring case; the alternative routes (closedness via internal hom; \texttt{sheafificationCompToSheaf} to \texttt{AddCommGrpCat}-side $W.\mathrm{IsMonoidal}$) are both blocked because \texttt{MonoidalClosed} for \texttt{PresheafOfModules}\,$R_0$ is absent for varying $R_0$ and \texttt{toPresheaf}\,$R_0$ is not monoidal (it sends a ring-balanced tensor to a $\mathbb Z$-tensor, which differs). The gap-fill remains the only outstanding piece of Phase~C step~C0; it does \emph{not} block downstream consumers, which use $\mathrm{MonoidalCategory}\,X.\mathrm{Modules}$ directly rather than $W.\mathrm{IsMonoidal}$ as a hypothesis.
61	\end{remark}
62	
63	\section{Invertible objects and line bundles}
64	
65	\begin{definition}[Invertible $\mathcal O_X$-module]
66	  \label{def:Modules_Invertible}
67	  An $\mathcal O_X$-module $L$ is \emph{invertible} (or a \emph{line bundle}) if there exists $L' \in X.\mathrm{Modules}$ and an isomorphism $L \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} L' \cong \mathcal O_X$.
68	\end{definition}
69	
70	Equivalently, $L$ is invertible iff it is an invertible object of the symmetric monoidal category $X.\mathrm{Modules}$ in the sense of \texttt{CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory.Invertible}.
71	
72	This is the substrate that Chapter~\ref{chap:Picard_LineBundle} is supposed to use to define $\mathrm{LineBundle}\,X$. The current chapter (still using the global-sections approximation $\mathrm{CommRing.Pic}\,\Gamma(X, \top)$) is forward-compatible with this refinement; the refinement is the content of Phase~C step~C1 (see \texttt{STRATEGY.md}).
73	
74	\section{Mathlib gap}
75	
76	Mathlib provides:
77	\begin{itemize}
78	  \item \texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory} on the presheaf side (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean}).
79	  \item \texttt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification} (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Sheafification.lean}) and \texttt{SheafOfModules.toSheafCompSheafToPresheafIso} as the comparison data.
80	  \item The generic \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.monoidalCategory} pattern for fixed-value monoidal categories.
81	\end{itemize}
82	
83	What is \emph{missing} and supplied by this chapter:
84	\begin{itemize}
85	  \item The instance \texttt{MonoidalCategory $X$.Modules} for $X$ a scheme. This is bookkeeping work over the existing presheaf-side instance.
86	  \item The invertibility predicate (Definition~\ref{def:Modules_Invertible}) and its API.
87	\end{itemize}
88	
89	\section{Use in the project}
90	
91	Theorem~\ref{thm:Modules_MonoidalCategory} unblocks:
92	\begin{itemize}
93	  \item Chapter~\ref{chap:Picard_LineBundle}, step~C1 of Phase~C: redefining $\mathrm{LineBundle}\,X$ as the type of invertible $\mathcal O_X$-modules (instead of the global-sections approximation).
94	  \item Chapter~\ref{chap:Picard_Functor}, step~C2: the relative Picard functor reformulated on the corrected $\mathrm{LineBundle}$.
95	  \item Chapter~\ref{chap:Picard_Functor}, step~C3: the representability theorem (FGA-style or via symmetric powers) is honestly stateable only after this refinement.
96	\end{itemize}
97	
98	\section{Formalization status}
99	
100	The Lean-side scaffolding lives in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Modules/Monoidal.lean} and carries the three signatures \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}, \texttt{Scheme.Modules.instMonoidalCategoryStruct}, and \texttt{Scheme.Modules.instMonoidalCategory}. The bodies are filled across successive prover rounds: the file is the active target of Phase~C step~C0 in the project strategy.
101
Continuing the audit — reading remaining chapters.

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes (Mumford \S4)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties}. It is the only sorry-blocking lemma in the project that is provable from current Mathlib alone, and it unlocks the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property).
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$ --- the source and target are abstract proper smooth geometrically irreducible group schemes over the base field $k$. It is therefore the highest-leverage standalone target for a prover round.
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X, Y$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible group schemes over $k$. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms of $k$-schemes, and let $U \subseteq X$ be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    g_1\big|_U \;=\; g_2\big|_U
17	  \]
18	  on the underlying topological points, then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms of $k$-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem usually states this for morphisms vanishing on an open subset (the difference morphism $g_1 - g_2$ vanishes on $U$, hence is identically zero). Our formulation above is the symmetric ``agree on $U$ $\Rightarrow$ agree everywhere'' version; it is interderivable with the standard statement using the group law on $Y$.
23	\end{remark}
24	
25	\section{Proof sketch}
26	
27	\begin{proof}
28	  \leanok
29	  Reduce to the standard form: let $\delta \colon X \to Y$ be the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, which makes sense because $Y$ carries a group-object structure. The hypothesis says $\delta$ takes the constant value $\eta[Y]$ (the identity of $Y$) on $U$.
30	
31	  Consider the equaliser
32	  \[
33	    E \;=\; \mathrm{eq}\bigl(\delta,\; (\mathtt{toUnit}\ X) \circ \eta[Y]\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
34	  \]
35	  i.e.\ the closed subscheme of $X$ on which $\delta$ agrees with the constant identity morphism. Two facts:
36	  \begin{enumerate}
37	    \item \emph{$E$ is closed in $X$.} The target $Y$ is separated over $\Spec k$ (proper $\Rightarrow$ separated), so the equaliser of any two morphisms into $Y$ is closed in the source.
38	    \item \emph{$E$ contains the open $U$.} By hypothesis on the underlying points; promote to a scheme-theoretic statement using the reduced-induced subscheme structure on $U$ and faithfulness of the underlying-set functor on reduced schemes.
39	  \end{enumerate}
40	  Since $X$ is geometrically irreducible, in particular irreducible, the closed subscheme $E$ contains a non-empty open $U$, hence equals all of $X$ as a topological space. Combined with the fact that both $X$ and $Y$ are reduced (smooth $\Rightarrow$ regular $\Rightarrow$ reduced), and the morphisms $\delta$ and $\mathtt{toUnit}\ X \circ \eta[Y]$ are determined by their values on the underlying topological space (because $X$ is reduced and $Y$ is separated), we conclude $\delta = \mathtt{toUnit}\ X \circ \eta[Y]$, i.e.\ $g_1 = g_2$.
41	
42	  \medskip
43	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
44	  \begin{itemize}
45	    \item Proper morphisms are separated.
46	    \item The equaliser-is-closed property for morphisms into a separated $k$-scheme.
47	    \item The underlying topological space of a geometrically irreducible scheme is irreducible.
48	    \item Reducedness for $X$ and $Y$, plus the lemma identifying scheme morphisms by their values on points (for reduced source and separated target).
49	    \item Smoothness implies regularity, hence reducedness.
50	    \item The group-object structure on $Y$ to form the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, and to access $\eta[Y]$.
51	    \item Mathlib's abelian-variety commutativity result is \emph{not} used in the proof, but provides the wider context (commutativity of the group law).
52	  \end{itemize}
53	\end{proof}
54	
55	\section{Use in the project}
56	
57	The rigidity theorem is used in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of the Jacobian). Specifically: given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, the rigidity theorem applied to $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to the symmetric power $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
58	
59	Note: the existence half of \ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
60	
61	\section{Mathlib gap}
62	
63	No rigidity lemma exists in current Mathlib. The closest pre-existing declaration is commutativity of proper geometrically integral group schemes (Stacks 0BFD). Our rigidity statement is independent of and in fact compatible with this commutativity result.
64	
65	The proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} uses only Mathlib infrastructure that is already present (separatedness from properness, equaliser closedness, irreducibility of source, reducedness from smoothness). It is therefore provable in this iteration.
66
